commentBody: Was stuck for a while on the Mrs. Adams clue and the "Went (for)" clue, and still haven't grasped why SOLD is the answer to 28D.
Someone who knows that Sigma is the sign for summation should also know that one of the Millennium problems has been solved (the Poincaré conjecture).
I had KEPL from crosses in the middle of 8 Down and was sure it had to be wrong! So fun!

To quote Mr. Ryan George, "super easy, barely an inconvenience".

I see we are back into the Great Easening after a promising challenge last week.  

/back into hiding

Well worth the extra squares. Nice one, Gene. 
I hope nobody leaves this one UNSOLVED. 
How many ASKING FOR A FRIEND quips will we see today?
I enjoyed this!  Thank you!

Probably TCS, but some sloppy editing here. Charcuterie does not mean cheese, such as BRIE. It means sliced meat.

FOOLSMATE is not a trap. It's more of an academic exercise of the shortest a game can end. It requires white to make two specific moves that would not be made under normal circumstances.

Contrast this to the Scholar's Mate or 4 move checkmate that can be done against a careless player who is making ordinary development moves without paying attention to what the opponent is doing.
Fun puzzle with a lot of really satisfying clues, but very easy for a Friday.  I set a PB at 15:35.

Not much is coming easy to me these days.   No issues with the puzzle, only with my recall.

It took me a while to work through the area around [Mrs. Addams, to Mr. Addams], until I remembered it was not John and Abigail Adams, but Gomez and Morticia.  

I sure could use an earth-like planet that isn't earth.

But, hey, I got 18A with no crosses!
Not keen on KGS for kilograms.  KG is much more usual.
Nice puzzle…for a Wednesday. Two minutes longer than my Friday best.

Not sure what's going on with replies. I got an email saying this reply posted, but I can't see it. Regarding whether BRIE is a [Charcuterie spread] in the U.S., not in France: https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/charcuterie
Anyone else thinking the column's title is a comment on the puzzle?
Very fun! Especially liked 'asking for a friend' and 'earthlike planet'.

This one was right up my alley. Loved the 15x16 grid with 3 spanners going across and the central 2 going down. Also was a very smooth solve with little hiccups to me. I remember his grid last December with One Of Everything.

It took me a while, very little came to me in the first pass, but eventually I solved it, not needing any hints or googling, and I thought the long entries were fun.  I enjoyed this puzzle.  Then I thought I'd check in to read the inevitable humble brags of how easy this puzzle was.  Could it be the puzzles are not easier, but you are just getting better at it?

Well, pretty easy for me but still oddly satisfying despite that and had a lot of interesting connections.
It’s not often I get the top row of a themeless off the bat—and I didn’t today as I tried AFRO before MESO—but I did pick BRIE right away (spread gave it away) and thanks to my daughter’s favorite phrase—“Sun’s out, guns out” —BICEPS.
I gotta say it—yesterday we had DIKDIKS and today Woodpeckers’ peckers—c’mon, talk about low hanging fruit!
Francis, like you I had Abby before Tish—thought I was so smart, I just couldn’t get their beautiful love letters quoted in “1776” out of my head. Then I couldn’t remember Morticia’s name and tried CARA his pet name for her (CARA MIA)
I always called “One Direction”, “One Dimension” (can’t stand boy bands) so now it’s confirmed. 
A philosopher famous for a razor right next to NAIR clued as manscaping? ROTFL
Two Chinese dogs, pet spa and ALLBARKANDNOBITE?
TASERS while streaking? That’s gotta hurt.
And finally, what a lovely unusual grid, couldn’t miss the double grid spanner down the middle and MOON next to EARTH? The crossword gods don’t get more heavenly than that.
So yeah, 15 minutes, which is a very quick Friday for me, but 15 minutes well spent with a smile on my face the whole time.
really well-constructed puzzle…thanks!
Very nice puzzle construction. Lots of satisfying solves. Quick note to point out that one of the Millenium problems has been solved.
Fun. Enjoyed the spanners. Thanks.

Ms De Vera must be a spiritual twin: I got all the spanners with very few crossers. Excellent long entries without gibberish fill, this was an impressive and satisfying puzzle for me.
Strangely, "Went (for)" was the clue that caused me most problems - took me a long while to finally make the 'auction' connection, and I've never seen the Addams Family in any incarnation...
Thanks Gene!

I expected to see some flying toys at the playground site, but it was all park and no kite.

(Sorry to string you along with that one.)
How refreshing to have ETD instead of ETA for once!

In "Moonlight Sonata," the sequel to "Red Planet," Val Kilmer reprised his role as Robby Gallagher, astronaut-engineer. This time he's sent to an earthlike planet in the Kal Sigma system, where many of the human immigrants were winding up dead. When no one hears from him again, no call home, no SOS signal, he's listed as AWOL.

Back on earth, they're glad, in a way, that Robby Gallagher's gone.  He was always hitting on the women, calling himself Iceman and referring to his biceps as his "top guns" and asking the women to give them a squeeze. "Asking for a friend," he'd say. In the open office, the women gossip how if he returned they'd all have to get tasers. Tish brands him "The Lost Luster." The mystery of what happened to him went unsolved for years.

Back in Kal Sigma, Robby is having the time of his life. The rich loess tastes like cakesicles, in fact, he eats so well he puts on many kgs. And the oak forests are practically a pet spa of peaceful animals, reminding him of pumas and chow-chows, but all bark and no bite. He finds the indigenous men folk to be all dolts and oafs.  And the women? "Only fools mate," Robby tells them, after inviting them to squeeze his top guns. "And I'm a fool." The women humor Robby, calling him K-Pop, bringing him drinks with delicious spices after they had seen to his more carnal needs, doing what they had always done: kill with kindness, then get out the mummy linen.

Yes Mr. De Vera, I did notice all the Ks in the puzzle –
and it's perfectly KK by me (à la my Gen Z niece's texting vocabulary...)

I also noticed how much joy I was having with the solve, albeit perplexed that I knew almost every answer. 
The few question marks were quickly resolved with some crossing letters (here's looking at you LOESS.)  The exception was  the Zillow clue for which I had RLS (Residential Listing Service) and the MATE part of the chess term just wasn't coming to me because I was parsing it as FOOL S_ATE.

I believe my ease in solving was either because I was on the same wavelength, or because this was Friday Lite, or because of Mr. De Vera's construction chops. 
One thing I do know for sure is that this was a very satisfying romp.

Thank you, Mr. De Vera!

I can't wrap my head around BRIE having anything to do with charcuterie - a charcutier is a cured/smoked/cooked meat vendor, and charcuterie is meat preparation in French cuisine. How does a cheese qualify as charcuterie? Also, Americans are the only ones to use brie as a spread - we already discussed this once on the board. Europeans eat brie cut into pieces, rind and all. Spreading it on a cracker (which I learned of in one of the previous puzzles) sounds blasphemous.

The cross of CAKE_ICLES and SO_ I only resolved by looking at the answer key. I could not parse the cake thing, and I wanted a sign of desperation to be a SOb 🤷🏽. I'm looking at cakesicles online as I'm writing this - I've never seen or heard of them before. 

MONT-Saint-Michel is a spectacular sight (but sadly also one of the most overcrowded tourist attractions I've ever visited... And yes, my wife and I contributed the problem). You just have to take care not to let the sea claim your car: part of the tourist car park is located in a tidal area 🫣

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
We appear to be back to CLOYingly easy Fridays.

LOESS the only Friday-level fill and a TIL, easily gotten by Tuesday-level crosses.

48D Mr. Ernst was long dead when Ernst & Whinney (formed when partners Ernst & Ernst merged with Whinney Smith) merged with the firm named for  Mr. Arthur Young, who was also dead.  Ernst and Young would have had to live well past 100 to see their names joined as partners.
Impressive construction! Thank you. The cluing didn't measure up to a Friday, though.
1 of the 7 Clay Millenium Problems has been  SOLVED.
Impressive spanners. Enjoyed discovering answers to all five as they revealed themselves. I will do my best to kill with kindness today..

I didn't find this puzzle so easy.  In fact, I had a hard time getting into it and found myself wandering in a desert of empty squares, until finally, one at a time, clues clicked and i had something to build on.  No look-ups were necessary, but some early guesses were pretty dumb.

Did anyone else have trouble solving it?  (Asking for a friend.)
What was up with the Mini today? Took me like 6 minutes and even then I had to look up answers. Crazy!
Weirdly, I was able to fill in the spans when I had completed very few of the other answers. The stars just lined up. I don't think I've ever done that before.
Middle left of the puzzle was the last to fall, for some reason those answers just didn't "click" it fell only after I got KILWITHKINDNESS.  But I did enjoy today's puzzle.
Fast and fun.

Well, now…

I woke up a bit “groggy” (hungover) 
from the feast hubby prepared last night-

a charcuterie spread, 
on a giant wooden slab,
complete with prosciutto, BRIE…

A veritable plethora of cheeses and meats,
jams, spreads, olives, fresh fruits…
and a bottle of Cabernet Sauvignon.

We chuckled realizing we dove into it
like our twenty year-old selves 
used to devour a cheap pizza after
smoking cheap weed.

The more things change,
the more they-

well, y’all know how it goes.

(But what’s with food hangovers? Whyyy??)

My favorite moment was when, while staring at the completed grid, my eyes fell on the abutting MOONLIGHT SONATA and EARTHLIKE PLANET, and for a moment I was whisked into “2001: A Space Odyssey”, specifically a segment where we, the viewers, are gliding through outer space to “The Blue Danube” waltz, and where stunningly, the splendor and majesty of the universe are viscerally felt in the bones.  

Where it’s clear that Earth, our home, and the focus of all our concerns, is but a speck in a vast universe, amidst a glorious and beautiful celestial dance.  Magic, truth, and beauty intertwined.  

Today, when I saw EARTHLIKE PLANET next to MOONLIGHT SONATA, there I was again, gliding through space, dollying toward and around the planet, amidst a starry background, accompanied this time by Beethoven’s haunting sonata, and it worked perfectly. A wow moment indeed.

Gene, your puzzle was fun to uncover, but even more so, it markedly lifted the quality of my day.  Thank you!

There were nine Ks in this one. So, fine pitching (or poor batting) today.

The 16 x 15 grid with the adjacent vertical spanners was interesting, and all five spanners were solid.

But the cluing here was disappointingly direct. As clued, this could almost have been a Tuesday puzzle. I was done in about fifteen minutes, and I'm hardly a fast solver. 

Once again I feel that a fine puzzle has been diminished by unfortunate editorial choices.
I really liked the three long spanners. This was a fun one.

So, if the cakesicle is eaten sticky-side-up, is it a stalacticake, and if sticky-side down, a stalagmacake?

(Asking for the fun of it.)

Well, typical tough Friday for me, with a couple of completely unfamiliar answers (notably - 37a) and others that were never going to dawn on me just from the clues. No big deal - that's just me. And... of course a puzzle find. A Sunday from August 18, 2019 by David Steinberg with the title "Revolutionary." Hard to describe this one, but what could be considered the 'reveal' was one long down answer: GETSTHEBALLROLLING. I'm not even going to try to describe it - I'll just provide the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/18/2019&g=49&d=D Hasta manana. ..

Excellent puzzle. Makes up for last Friday’s absurdity. 

But I still don’t understand why streakers are “charged” with TASERS?
A pleasant challenge.  Nicely done and thanks.

Well....Clever puzzle BUT I still have half my cup of coffee left here!  Now, doggone it, what am I supposed to do?  

I guess I can while away some time looking up "Guns, so to speak" and how it could possibly mean BICEPS, but my will to live has been sapped.
Okay, I Googled it.  Big whoop.

We had torrential rains pounding much of the night.  The mosquitos are gonna go nuts!  And speaking of NUTS, do NOT drive into standing water.  Please.

Any chance the nyt website can provide a clickable link to all the previous puzzles of a crossword constructor? 

I'm working my way through the archives (still in 1992,) but when a current puzzle catches my fancy, I like to look at the previous efforts of the same person.

Appropriate, then, that some of the scenes in in the 1938 film The  Adventures Of Robin Hood were also filmed in a 16A, though different species--California's Q. lobata, Q. douglassii, and Q. wislizeni not  the English Oak Q. robur.  

 In the movie some of the tree leaves were spray painted green, because the movie was filmed in Technicolor.

Nice puzzle, well-constructed, enjoyed it. Thanks, Gene!

Sometimes in Friday and Saturday puzzles, I'm thrown for a loop by what we might call a false mis-direction:
a clue and answer that are straight-forward but I go off on tangents because I'm expecting some sly trickiness.
Maybe newer solvers don't get caught this way?

Today, it was 36A  Genre for Blackpink (whoever they are). 
K__P
Oh, it must be some Korean niche genre. 
K-RAP?
K-HOP?
K-BOP?
The obvious K-POP didn't even occur to me until the downs filled it in.

All POP and no snap or crackle, in that clue/answer.

I enjoyed this puzzle. But I agree with a poster below that a charcuterie platter holds a variety of cured meats. When you add brie, it becomes a charcuterie-cheese platter.  If you shop at a charcuterie in France, you won't find brie.

Enjoyed the puzzle! But I do have a couple of nits to pick...

1-Across: The clue should be singular, since the answer - biceps - is actually singular; the plural is bicepses. (I kid you not!)

11-Across: Yes, cheese can be considered part of a charcuterie board in the U.S and U.K.  But in France, charcuterie only refers to meats.

Words and phrases in their original language often acquire different meanings and usages when adopted by a different language. I never understood how "entrée," meaning "first course" in French, came to mean "main course" in American English, when the word is so clearly evocative of a beginning. And plurals are often a problem in translation: for example, "panini" is already plural in Italian - the singular is "panino" - but is used as a singular in English. Oh, well...
A decent Friday puzzle, no marginalia.

Weird. PB for a Friday, but it didn’t seem unusually easy; I was lucky enough to get boosts from getting the spanners quickly with few crossers, and some clues people here have said were tough came to me right away.

I thought that despite the eponymous razor, it was William of Ockham, but after looking it up, I learned that Occam is an acceptable spelling of the place, not just said razor. Makes sense since spelling rules were relatively loose then, compared to recent centuries.
That was like, so tough but also easy? I think I was unsure about almost every fill except KILMER but still beat my average time.
Not me giggling uncontrollably when my knowledge of music and astronomy finally came into use with 7 and 8 Down.

This one went along smoothly. Helped that the spanners came to me easily. Really liked those and they seemed to fit together, made this kind of a 'themed themeless' puzzle for me.
Very good, bravo.

“K” polooza! 

I like it when “K” pops up in a word. It’ s my initial which, for some reason, makes me smile.  Even better, it’s a less common letter and with a distinctive click to it, a great tickler for my internal dictionary of crossword answers.  

Once I noticed the abundance, the puzzle fell very quickly.
I found today's puzzle much easier than yesterday's; came in quite a bit below my average. YMMV enjoyed the puzzle--thanks
My kind of Friday! Makes up for last week and I didn’t feel like a complete DOLT! 😆

One more puzzle find. A Tuesday from July 29, 2008 by David Kwong and Emily Halpern. Four 15 letter theme answers in that one: THEDECENTGATSBY GOODBARRIERREEF THENOTBADESCAPE OKAYBALLSOFFIRE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/29/2008&g=65&d=A ...
What a colorful crossword. Loved it!
Breezy, fun puzzle, but I think it's worth stating that any officer who tases a streaker should lose their badge and perhaps face assault charges. Tasers can kill; they are for emergencies.
Lively puzzle, but I'm brain-locking on 26-Down. How is 'Is' a clue that means 'means'?

12% quicker than average rather than 20% because had to spend 5 minutes locating my error: SOb instead of SOS.  Had no idea what 3D was supposed to be, but it jumped out once I ran the alphabet at 23A and got to the "S".  Had a very slow start, but once I got a few of the spanners I really picked up speed.  Good challenge.  


My time was 12% quicker than average rather than the 20% I would have had if I hadn't spent 5 minutes locating my error:  SOb instead of SOS.  Son of a ...  

Had no idea what 3D was supposed to be, but it jumped out once I ran the alphabet at 23A and got to the "S".  The first pass yielded very little, but once a couple of spanners came to mind, I really picked up speed.  

Good challenge.
This was a very fun solve!
Reminds me of a Robin Weintraub puzzle with lots of good natured fill. On the easier side for me but fun.

Sometimes you're not on the constructor's wavelength and you slog (this can be especially tough on Friday and Saturday) and sometimes you are and things go nice and smooth. Today was a nice and smooth: not quite a PB but well under half my Friday average.
With clues like these on a Friday, why not just go half a step further and literally give you the answer? What's the point?

I solved the difficulty of 11-Across by adding a nice Pâté to my charcuterie.  Personally I love a nice Pâté de campagne, and it's very spreadable.

And it fit so nicely, not only with ET(a/D)S, but also with PEEL for "Zest" at 11-Down.

BRIE is boring.  If we're adding cheese, I'd prefer a Camembert.  
And BRIO?
Didn't we just see you two days ago with your friend CON?

Maybe it's frowned upon in this space but I wish more people put their solve time in the comments, especially those complaining that the puzzle is too easy or too hard. 

I thought this was on the easier side for a Friday and got the gold star in 9:02, which is about half my all-time average for Friday (18:38).

Fun Friday though [for me] on the easy side. I had been going to say about 3D that where I come from they are CAKEPOPS, but a bit of research turned up that they are slightly different and allegedly less likely to fall off their sticks than the POPS. Starbucks used to sell the POPS..haven't seen them lately but haven't been there much lately either.  Several of us once surprised a friend with them after she got an award...yes they are silly @Andrzej et all, but it was an easy way to create a festive occasion.
What happened to the MIDI puzzles?

This one really clicked for me. My fastest Friday at a little over half my average (admittedly a small sample as I don't usually get a gold star on Fridays yet). Those long entries are so fun to fill, especially when you guess them with no cross help. I feel like a lot of folks will have found this to be "the easiest Friday everrr" etc, but for me it was a chance to feel like I might eventually be able to solve the harder Fridays. Thanks for the boost!
Here's another Friday for those of you who might want a bit more of a challenge: https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2020/05/29
Horrible yet again, so many incorrect clues

Mr. McGowan, as a very long-time solver (occasional poster), I want to let you know how much I appreciate your humor and insights.

Keep up the good work, no pressure!

I've been doing crosswords for a couple of years now but only now felt compelled to comment... Basically, I am extremely disappointed at that BRIE/BRIO cross in the top right corner. Even as I was filling it in I thought: "no, it can't possibly be that, they wouldn't reuse 75% of a 4-letter word in a cross with itself, it can't be....oh no. It is."
I did notice all the K's! What a lovely puzzle. 18ish minutes for me today.
At 11:27 it's my fastest Friday yet. And on my little phone. Like butter.

Although I dutifully filled in SKA for [Calypso cousin]--humming The English Beat while I did--I really wanted the answer to be PYRRHA*. If the BRIE on your charcuterie board is really a spread, it means it has been left at room temperature way too long, and I would advise against the soppressata. *I confess, I had to look this up in Grimal. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jgsO1FxlWUU&list=RDjgsO1FxlWUU&start_radio=1

I'm surprised no one took issue with 57 across.  "More sizzle than steak" is when something is overhyped. "All bark and no bite" refers to someone who talks aggressively but doesn't act. They aren't close enough to be clue/answer to me.
we rarely get Saturdays with this much fun word play panache and genuinely sterling clueing/answers bravo Gene - this is a classic

Wonderful puzzle, solved under my average time, and full of delightful clues and interesting facts. I thoroughly enjoyed it. 

I have a very small nit to pick about 11A, and I know that American usage is the issue for sure. But a charcuterie board would NEVER include cheese. The word "charcuterie" literally means pork products, usually dried varieties like salami/sausage, and pâtés. It is garnished with cornichons and other pickled veggies, and occasionally with nuts. There is almost always mustard too, especially if there is pâté.

I know that these are clues, not definitions, but this "clue" is way off the mark and it's a pet peeve of mine! 🤷🏼‍♀️

TIL that Linon is an actual type of fabric and not just a wrong entry in my puzzling day. It’s a weave of linen and cotton. 

Dogspa/Linon worked just fine, but PETSPA/LINEN is correct.
Nobody told me there'd be math! :)
I think EARTHLIKEPlANET is the first time I’ve ever got a full spanner without needing any crosses. Good ol’ Kepler 22b.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ manscaping the millenium
with lavish peace-speak and a charcuterie
board of d/ sticky data treats…. i say, the
barriers to pleasure heard you shout my
birthday as the soil bleeding sweetness became
the sacred saps of fantaisie — inspiration for
the wedding party!
or became the a/ forest asking for its moonlight

commentBody:

just some easy thinking got me the solve, and a few smart clues made it fun

happy friday, everyone!

Thanks, Lou Scheffer and B! That meaning escaped my ken.

@Lou Scheffer

The whole puzzle had sloppy clueing. I identified two more
And six remain UNSOLVED. Are you suggesting the crossword clue needed to have mentioned that one was solved?

@Barry Ancona

"Like most of the Millennium..." would have worked just fine and been more precise clueing
It would have worked just fine and would have been more precise. I don't think the precision was needed.
@Barry Ancona, I'd suggest "Like most Millenium problems".
I like that. Swap one word for another.

@Barry Ancona

Yes.  Or qualified with "most".  The group comprising "the Millennium Problems" cannot be described as unsolved, it's simply not true.  Would you also describe the members of the Beatles as "dead"?

@Lou Scheffer

Good point, @Lou Scheffer.
@Lou Scheffer I don't think so; summation is taught in high school, the millennium problems usually not.

The clue reads "Like the Millennium Problems of the Clay Mathematics Institute", for the answer UNSOLVED.

In fact, one of the problems was solved over 20 years ago.*

Therefore the clue is in error.

* This was a very dramatic episode. Starting in 2003, Russian mathematician Grigori Perelman posted three papers proving the Poincaré conjecture from 1904 (which asserted that the only compact simply connected three-dimensional manifold is the three-sphere). When the mathematics community had deemed then to be valid proofs, Perelman was awarded the Fields medal, one of the highest honors in mathematics, which he declined to accept. Books have been written about these events.

@B

Although I disagree a bit with the conclusion, I do love Ryan George.

commentBody:
@B

The Great Easening would be a fine product name for a laxative.

Steven,
We're not in France. "In the USA, 'charcuterie' is a misnomer, featuring a selection of preserved foods, especially cured meats or pâtés, as well as cheeses and crackers or bread." (wiki)

I'll leave your other point for the chess team.

@Barry Ancona

So if the definition you cite calls it a misnomer, I would expect the paper of record to not use it in their crossword clueing without acknowledging it as such
Steven, The "misnomer" is in the dictionary. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/charcuterie
Steven, The "misnomer" is in the dictionary. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/charcuterie
@Barry Ancona. “Charcuterie”  is a misnomer in America just as “entree” is when used for “main courses. Or rather, we are dealing with with polysemy with loan becoming naturalised.

@Steven M.

I suspect you are right on concerning the two-move mate is not actually a "trap" in a chess sense.

@Barry Ancona

Interesting. So MW defines Charcuterie as strictly meat while Charcuterie Board is defined as meat plus things that compliment the meat (cheese, bread, nuts, pickles, etc)

@Steven M.
It's a "misnomer" in that the literal translation in the source language doesn't apply. But it's still correct American usage. And the puzzle is American.
@Steven M.  TCS? What does that mean?
@Steven M. Thank you and thanks to Steve L. as well. That’s a very useful term!
@Steven M. "Charcuterie board" would have been a clearer clue but I got it right away.  The CBs I've seen always have nonmeat items such as bread, cheese, and fruit.

@Barry Ancona

The clue is even more confusing than that to a European. Not only is our charcuterie about meat only, we also don't use brie as a spread. That clue is the most American one I have ever seen.
@Barry Ancona Some of us are in France actually. But don’t worry, we’re used to it.

@Cindy
Thanks for asking that question.  Hope I can remember it!

Having lived in France for a year once, I recall that charcuterie does not comprise cheeses, so this clue is at least the second one yesterday that is in error (along with the Millennium Problems clue). Add to that the Fool's Mate error, and indeed there are three janky clues. (Add to that a recent clue asserting that feral cats are not housebroken ...)

@Cindy

Too close syndrome. A phrase invented by fellow commentator Steve L to indicate that one someone has professional or personal experience in a subject, they'll object to clueing that would seem perfectly acceptable to a lay person.

In my case, I was an avid chess player in my youth and am an even more avid diner in my adulthood

@Steven M.

Fools mate sure felt like a trap when it was used on me for the first time, and likewise when I later was able to use it on a few unsuspecting players.  Yes we were young and not very experienced players, but that doesn't mean it wasn't a trap.
The reason that Fool's Mate is not a trap is that there is nothing anyone can do to set such a trap for their opponent. The victim of Fool's Mate has to make just the right wrong moves.

@Dan

That is true of traps.

Sure, it's not rare for that to happen.  Ironically, well done!

@Philly Carey

So, your suggestion comes up frequently and, to be honest, it’s insulting to our intelligence to assume we don’t take that into consideration (whether you agree or disagree). Of course we have. But first, there are people who have been solving for 30+ years or more, it seems unlikely that they suddenly have become more experienced in the last 5 years (no offense, but it’s more likely they would have gotten a bit more senile, I know I have). Second, I’ve only been solving the NYT about 6 years, so perhaps there could be a trend for me—but when I do archived puzzles from 10 years ago, I see a huge difference. And it’s not explained by cultural references, because I am generally more familiar with those of a decade ago than today’s.

@Philly Carey

I have to say that SP, along with others here, convinced me.  The more puzzles I sample from the archives, the more I can see that it's rare for me to get a Friday or Saturday (or even Sunday) from 20 years ago.

But, and here's another problem, one that I've had.

Nothing of any value will come from constantly scanning the posts and trying to establish your ranking.  First, there is no ranking.  And second, what difference could it possibly make if there were?  This is not our living.  Almost nobody's paying the mortgage with their crossword skills.

You'll have more fun on the forum if you consider us companions rather than competitors.

@Philly Carey

One more thing… I wish the conversation could get redirected from “are crosswords getting easier?” (which to me seems undeniable) to “is that possibly a good thing?” which is a much more interesting question, and one for which I can see support on both sides. In the one hand, perhaps we have just become a much more immediately self-gratifying society, and crosswords need to evolve to satisfy that, like many other media. Maybe as a whole many people can’t be expected to appreciate a puzzle anymore which has to simmer over an hour or overnight to be solved. And frankly if I have to choose between the current level of difficulty and none at all I’ll certainly take the former. On the other hand, I can’t help but feel that we could still be the last bastion for savoring a good challenge, and that if newer solvers stick with it they will see the value of it and gradually improve their skills and not jump to Google every 5 minutes. But in a society used to AI, streaming, Twitter (I know, X now, even that was too long), instathis snd instathat, and immediate internet access to anything and everything, that’s a lot to ask.

@Philly Carey, one thing you can say objectively is that, for subscribers to the xwstats website (arguably not a representative sample, but still a signal), this puzzle was easier than the typical Friday:

Difficulty: Easy
Median Solve Time: 11:09
Median Solver: 27% faster
⚡87% of users solved faster than their Friday average.
  63% solved much faster (>20%) than their Friday average.
🐢13% of users solved slower than their Friday average.
  2% solved much slower (>20%) than their Friday average.

If I may offer my own data point, this was my fastest Friday ever, taking sligthly less than half my average time, and falling somewhere between my Tuesday and my Wednesday averages. Subjectively, it did feel like somewhere between Tuesday and Wednesday in terms of how many entries I was able to fill on my first pass.

I'm not complaining. I thought it was a very nice grid, and I particularly enjoyed seeing the debut entries ASKING FOR A FRIEND and FOOLS MATE (even though I agree with others that it is more of a hypothetical than a real trap).

@Striker

Are you, ad absurdum and Ace all the same person?

@Steve L

I don’t *think* so. 

But in the movies the split personalities seldom seem to know about “the others”, so, not entirely impossible? 

PS- There’s a comedy in there somewhere. Modern twist on the split personality thriller but instead of a psycho killer each “other self” is just a different contributor on the same forum.

Lots of political metaphor opportunity. “Left wing,  right wing, same bird” kinda vibe.

@SP

Your comment, "simmer over an hour or overnight to be solved", just made me think of a reason this might be the case that I hadn't considered before - the streak. The gamification.

For people to keep their streak, they have to finish the same day, right? It can't simmer overnight anymore if they want the gold star. NYT Games has decided to lean into gamification with this stuff and this seems to be a pretty natural consequence of that that I hadn't considered before.

It kinda bums me out. Not criticizing anyone who cares about their streak or NYT Games for this choice, really, but I'm just a bit... bummed. Not sure I can fully articulate it. I just really like enjoying things for what they are without having something hijacking that part of my brain, I guess. It's crept into so many parts of it lives.

@SP

As a counter to that I’m a relatively new enthusiast of the NYT crossword. I have been trawling back through the archive, currently on late 2014 and my best times are all in puzzles between 2015 and 2016.

@Wyra

Here in New York, the next day's puzzle is published at 10 pm (along with the Midi and the Mini). So lots of good simmering.

@Tony

Wow. I respect that but it is counter to my experience. Is it just that your best times are there, or is that an overall impression? Because there are occasionally easy puzzles there too, of course, it just seems like overall they are harder, and the hardest puzzles are much harder than the hardest today.

@SP I’m very sympathetic to those who are disappointed by the gradual eneasyfication of the puzzle—it must be frustrating to lose that satisfying challenge. On the other hand, selfishly, I am personally glad they’re easier because I have a lot to do in a day and I rarely want to spend more than x minutes on a crossword puzzle, where x is the usual amount I do spend these days and about half of what I used to spend five years ago when I spared the time, which was not every day. I can see why so many of the commenters here appear to be retired and to have plenty of leisure for puzzling—fantastic for them and I look forward to those days!

@SP

I did, and I'm really glad! I'm still playing through it as well. Lots of game to play through and enjoy!

@SP

I love some of the songs from "1776". Have you ever seen an actual production? I haven't.

@ad absurdum

There’s a movie version—which is Ok—and I have seen a community theater production. It’s a very well done musical, but not so well known, I think.

@Alex

You are from Peru so may not know, and I certainly don’t know Gene’s gender preference, but typically Gene is short for Eugene which is a male name. Jean or Jeanne is the preferred spelling for females (although certainly not in France for Jean, which just shows you how tricky it can be across cultures!)

@SP my grandmother's name was Gene, short for Genevieve. Perhaps the fact that this constructors middle name is Louise might offer a clue. While it is possible, typically men don't have the middle name Louise. Given that you are from wherever you are from, perhaps you don't know that.

@SP
FWIW, xwordinfo.com refers to the constructor as Mr. De Vera.

¡Ay qué vergüenza! Many apologies to Mr De Vera, if that's the correct title. Perhaps I can pretend it was a typo and I meant to write "Mx". Whoever and wherever they are, we were on the same wavelength in this puzzle!
Yikes. I probably won't be seen in this comment section for a while...

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Alex

Disappearing would be an extreme and unnecessary reaction, indeed.

You are not the first to have misguessed a setter's or a poster's gender.

I speak from experience.

And you will not be the last.

@Alex

If it's any consolation - I have always thought Gene Louise was female. I mean, Gene can be anything, and I've only ever seen Louise as a woman's name.

@Andrzej
Joan Miró, Rainer Maria Rilke...

@Ned S

 No just noticed the Louise myself, so you  are right about that. I was trying to be polite about it…

@Mike

Reel funny line.
@Mike i love your breezy humor!

@Mike

What kind of yoyo are you anyway?

@Mike
Maybe the flying toys were boxed.  (That one's a diamond in the rough.)

@john ezra

Bravo! 👏👏👏

@john ezra 
Wow! Impressive!

@Andrzej in North America (well, maybe not Mexico or Central America), the word charcuterie is used for a meal (it can be served as an appetizer, comprise the entire meal, or served after or in between meals) that can consist of breads, crackers, meats, cheeses, spreads, fruit/veg, nuts, and sweets or any combination of those.

@Nik

Thank you. By now I have gathered as much from the posts below but I always appreciate it when the members of the community explain the cultural context of these puzzles to me 🙂

I understand how words acquire different meanings in different languages spoken by people with distinct cultures. It gets confusing though if you are intimately familiar with another meaning already. I've traveled all over France many times over the decades, and French culinary arts with their terminology - which form an important part of French national identity - have become ingrained in my mind.

Over here, we call a meat and cheese platter... A meat and cheese platter 😃. A typical Polish dish of this kind features a selection of ham (szynka), sausages (kiełbasa), brined cucumbers (ogórki kiszone), artisanal cheese (ser - recently Poland has been enjoying an incredible Renaissance of local artisanal cheese production, which disappeared completely under "communism") and horseradish (chrzan):

@Andrzej 
Great, now I'm hungry.
@Andrzej if you allow the Brie to ooze then scooping it up on a cracker can be yummy. 😀

@Francis

Could we have dikdik-themed cakesicles?

@NanuNanu

Food exists, so you're golden :D

@Andrzej
Wow, so interesting to see how close the Polish word for horseradish is to Yiddish (chrain), but then I shouldn't be at all surprised. But chrain didn't make it into the Spelling Bee from the other day.

@Times Rita

Knowing chutzpah (the Polish hucpa/chucpa) helped me in Spelling Bee on Monday. In fact, I got my first ever Queen Bee that day, albeit with help of the forum hints for two words I was missing - one of them was the other Jewish term, chuppah.

Ιασων,

Mmmmm… forbidden (in Europe) briesicles!
mmmm…..
@Andrzej I celebrate a holiday I made up called CharcuterChristmas, where I make 4-6 themed charcuterie boards and drink pairings :-) we really run with things here!
@Andrzej To me Brie is a cheese, which when ripe becomes soft enough to be almost spreadable but it is it no way a 'spread'. Whatever beast it is I now want some!

@Andrzej

those'd be cakedikles.

@Andrzej When charcuterie (in your sense of the word) is served with cheese, farinaceous tidbits, and condiments, it's traditionally called a charcuterie board. Using the word to mean the board has come into usage over time.

Try Mount Saint Michael in Cornwall for a less hyped experience. It's a virtual duplicate. And while you're there, check out the Tate Modern in St. Ives. Altogether, Cornwall is one of my favorite places on the planet.

@Matt It's appeared many times before, also quite recently, IIRC. I know of LOESS (or "less", as we spell it - it's one of the few Polish words with a double "s") because it famously provides fertility to agricultural land in the Lubelszczyzna region of Poland, and LOESS erosion LED to the creation of the picturesque ravines of the tourist hot spot of Kazimierz Dolny, one of Poland's prettiest towns: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kazimierz_Dolny

commentBody:

I like to think I missed those fills since they  serendipitously appeared on crosses and I CBA'ed to read every clue if I've already heard the music.

I really don't like to think (Dr. Francis will give me an AMEN here) I actively acknowledged it, got it from crosses, read about it, said "Hey, that's pretty cool" to my cat (she likes to learn stuff, too) and since forgot it.

And, finally, aren't you special with your Lubechachacha region and your pretty Polish towns.

commentBody:
@Michael

...sophomoric, but there was an old riddle:  What large accounting firm sounds like both ends of a horse? See above for answer...

@Michael

I had a feeling that something was fishy with 48D. I guess if you consider Ernst and Young as words not actual people the clue works but it's still pretty lame. It makes me wonder if this clue was researched at all.

@dutchiris

I had a similar experience. Some of the entries (loess, cakesicles) were unknown to me, some required multiple guesses (dog spa, no, cat spa, no, pet spa), and I lacked confidence throughout. I got there eventually, but it wasn’t by any kind of wavelength.
@dutchiris It's funny how these things work. I had no trouble at all – guess some days you're just on the same wavelength with the constructors and editor \_(ツ)_/
@dutchiris I got about 40% through and then hit a wall. The clues weren’t the best today imo.

@dutchiris

I almost always have *some* trouble these days.  Words that simply will not come to mind, words are no are not the answer but keep coming back to mind.
@Frankie B a bad day perhaps? I don’t usually bother with the mini or midi (not that much of an addict) but prompted by your comment I had a go. Under a minute which is a tad slow but not by much.

@Frankie B

It was for the birds!
And I loved it.  
Quick solve, 41 seconds, even though I'm hunting and pecking around all the clutter on my desk.

Just not your wavelength today, I guess.

@CCNY

And then....the bill came.

Raw onion, not so much.  So, so unfair!

  ♠
5

@Lewis

POWERSUIT

Lewis,
I read the "5" as the clue number and the spade symbol as a superscript.

Xword Junkie,
With all the [delightful] spanners, I'm not sure kicking up the fill clues a notch would have made much of a difference. I renew my call to make Wednesday a swing day: sometimes a harder themed puzzle, sometimes an easy themeless like this one, sometimes a "fun but light" trick puzzle like yesterday's.
@Down_Home Oops, I mean five spanners – well done!
@JohnWM I lost a few seconds here for assuming the spelling was CAKECICLES, y’know, as in icicles, rather than CAKESICLES as in POPSICLES.  I guess that’s on me for snacking in fruit and nuts, huh?

Dan,

@Mark P

I struggled as well. In the UK streakers are normally tackled rugby style by security staff if lucky. Otherwise the competing sportspeople take them down!
@G I did read it, I still don’t understand the use of CHARGE as a verb there and thought I was missing something. For me you just “tase” someone with a taser

😎

@Lara
Thank you!
@McT It was filmed in Chico in Bidwell Park (wonderful place) and my grandfather drove one of the trucks. He was thrilled to meet Errol Flynn. Big story in his life.
We discussed this last night (while eating BRIE). We're in the USA, where a "charcuterie platter" is not just meat. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/charcuterie

@LStott

I think everyone is missing that Brie, well ripe Brie, as it should be, is spreadable!

Tony,
That, too, would be disputed in France.

@Barry Ancona

I lived in France for many years. Plenty of ripe Brie, and few pretensions about how it should be served or eaten!

Tony,
Preference, not pretension.

@Barry Ancona

Well quite, hence the complete lack of disputes!

Tony,
Whether we are in France, the U.K., or the U.S., I hope we agree that ripe BRIE need not be runny, and if it isn't runny it isn't spreadable.
@LStott What people do in France needn’t have much bearing on an American crossword.

Jonathan, 1A: I wondered if anyone would suggest that BICEPS *should* have a singular clue. It certainly *could* have a singular clue, but BICEPS is also fine as the plural. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/biceps 11A: Amen.

@SBS

Yeah not sure exactly what they were going for on that one other than really stretching the clue to include the word play.
@SBS There's no denying the fact that some streakers have gotten tased, but I agree that it is excessive force, potentially deadly, and not something I would want to make fun of.

@SBS
Amen.
Speaking of emergencies, did any of you feel any unease about yesterday's "metaphorical" BATTLE SCARS?

@Barry Ancona

Yes.

@LordBottletop
It is what it is.
[Is] would work for MEANS here: to have importance to the degree of Health means everything. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/mean

@Jim

Oops.  Heavily editing can introduce a bunch of mistakes. The intent was not to include the first paragraph.

@Hugh

It's not frowned upon, but it's not particularly helpful either. Solve time is a function of personal speed and ease of solve. One person's 15 minutes is not the same as another person's 15 minutes. For some people, that's slow. For others, that's fast

@Hugh
Well I took about an hour. I found it difficult. And?
Hugh, If you're interested in solve times, look here: https://xwstats.com/

@Hugh

I agree that solve times are interesting  and expect the constructor would like to see them too.

My time today was 24:02. That's a bit quick for me on a Friday. The "old" Fridays used to take me an hour. The hard, "old" Fridays used to take me an hour and a half.

My issue with the xword stat app is that it's invasive (for me) and likely a non-representative sample.

@Hugh
I don't use the timer.  The more thought-provoking puzzles take longer, just the way I like them, and an active bird feeder can add several minutes I'm sure.  I thought this puzzle was somewhat interesting and fairly easy.  Probably not Wednesday easy nor tricky enough for a Thursday and definitely no Saturday.  Goldilocks says "Friday!"

@Renegator The sample of those choosing to share their times in this forum on a daily basis would be a far more unrepresentative sample (not to mention there would be no way of verifying the accuracy of that data).

@Hugh

I happened to put it in today just to say it was a fun 15 minutes, but I rarely do and wish people wouldn’t. First I don’t speed solve and sometimes like to linger over interesting clues, so a longer solve for me could still be pretty easy. And conversely the people who consistently get really fast times must be pretty fast typists. So it’s pretty meaningless to me, like to hear whether people thought it easier or harder but other than that I don’t think it’s very helpful.

@Renegator

How is it invasive for you? They only record your times if you sign up and link to the NYT.

@Lin

Well, it would presumably be all the people using xwordstats plus some others.

@Jane Wheelaghan

It's interesting.

Huh? Why on earth would you think all the people using xword would come here to share their times? I’m sure many/most of them don’t use this forum at all and of those who do many/most wouldn’t be interested in dropping by every day to manually share their time.

@Lin

You might be right, but if they are interested in the stats and more people posted their times here, then they might come by more often and once here, post their times.

@Renegator

So you mean it would theoretically be invasive if you chose to give xwstats.com access to your stats. Yet the NYT already has your stats. The NYT could simply choose to publish those stats themselves. No one would know anyone’s individual stats in that scenario, and similarly, if you chose to share your data with xwstats, only you would know what your stats are. 

And it’s not as if they’re state secrets. 

But since you didn’t agree to sharing your stats (I assume), how can it actually be invasive to you in this life?

@Steve L

My understanding is that you give them access to your account.

@RozzieGrandma
Cake pops are rolled in a ball before baking, CAKESICLES are poured into a mold. (My niece had the molds on her wish list last Christmas.) I've never had one of either - just give me a cupcake.
"Leave the CAKESICLES. Take the cannoli."
@Mary Benner Still there, but the link can be glitchy.
Mary, I caught one! Help me reel it in. https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/midi

@Mary Benner
If you don't see it, try refreshing the browser.  I did have to do that every day until I did not have to.

@Mary Benner
I use the app and have never ever had an issue. Since we are talking MIDI my goal is 2 minutes which  have achieved only twice (both around 1:30) since its inception.
@Barry Ancona This is a great one. Really miss when the puzzles were up to this standard

Dc,
Other than 47A, which probably should read [Like most...] instead of [Like the...], which clues did you deem incorrect?

Irina,
I'm sorry you found BRIE/BRIO extremely disappointing. I found it rather amusing. Different strokes...

@Irina 

It's just a reminder that one should eat one's BRIE with BRIO!

@Irina

There’s no rule being violated here. Some don’t want to see similarly spelled words near each other, while others delight in this type of occurrence. As long as the two words don’t share a common etymology, there’s nothing wrong with it; it’s just not your preference.

@Barry Ancona

Pretty condescending.
@Vaer Too cute
Different strokes. Our Wordplay columnist called the 57 Across clue and answer idioms and synonyms.
@Elbridge Gerry They have differences but they also have commonalities: not having the substance to back up their style. A perfectly sound clue/answer pair for me.

Susan,
After several of us explained that [charcuterie] in the U.S. and the U.K. has a broader meaning than in France, at dinner tonight at a French restaurant I started with the charcuterie, which, of course, did not include BRIE.
@Susan E The clue isn’t wrong. In America, charcuterie boards often include cheese.
dUMAS and TOgS are indeed words, but not for the given clues!
Truth!
What's the problem? It was fine if left UNSOLVED.
